steps.explanation: After checking with peek, use next() to actually consume and add the value. peek doesn't advance the iterator; next does.
steps.code: sum += iter.next().unwrap();

challenges.title: Partition Split
challenges.description:
Use `partition` to split a collection. Write a function that takes a slice of numbers and returns two Vecs: even numbers and odd numbers. Use the partition method.

Expected output:
Evens: [2, 4, 6]
Odds: [1, 3, 5]
challenges.hint: Use iter().partition(|x| x % 2 == 0). Elements satisfying the predicate go to the first collection, others to the second.

steps.title: Understand partition
steps.explanation: partition splits an iterator into two collections based on a predicate. True values go to the first, false to the second.
steps.code: nums.iter().partition(|x| predicate)

steps.title: Clone for owned values
steps.explanation: iter() gives &i32. To get Vec<i32>, use cloned() to convert &i32 to i32. partition then collects into two Vecs.
steps.code: nums.iter().cloned().partition(|x| x % 2 == 0)

steps.title: Return type inference
steps.explanation: The return type (Vec<i32>, Vec<i32>) tells partition what to collect into. partition works with any type implementing Default + Extend.

challenges.title: Chain Link
challenges.description:
Use `chain` to concatenate iterators. Write a function that takes two slices and returns a Vec containing all elements from both, with the first slice reversed. Use chain and rev.

Expected output:
[3, 2, 1, 4, 5, 6]
challenges.hint: Use first.iter().rev().chain(second.iter()).cloned().collect(). chain concatenates two iterators into one.

steps.title: Reverse the first iterator
steps.explanation: rev() reverses a double-ended iterator. first.iter().rev() yields elements in reverse order.
steps.code: first.iter().rev()

steps.title: Chain with second iterator
steps.explanation: chain() takes another iterator and produces elements from the first, then from the second. It concatenates without allocating intermediate collections.
steps.code: .chain(second.iter())

steps.title: Convert to owned and collect
steps.explanation: cloned() converts &i32 to i32. collect() gathers into a Vec. The entire operation is lazy until collect forces evaluation.
steps.code: .cloned().collect()

challenges.title: Zip Merge
challenges.description:
Use `zip` to pair up elements. Write a function that takes two slices of equal length and returns a Vec of tuples. Also write a function using zip to compute dot product of two vectors.

Expected output:
[(1, 4), (2, 5), (3, 6)]
32
challenges.hint: zip pairs elements from two iterators. For dot product: zip, map to multiply pairs, then sum.

steps.title: Zip two iterators
steps.explanation: zip() pairs up elements from two iterators. It stops when either iterator is exhausted, so it handles different lengths safely.
steps.code: a.iter().zip(b.iter())

steps.title: Transform pairs
steps.explanation: map converts each (&i32, &i32) pair. For pair_up, dereference to get (i32, i32). For dot_product, multiply the values.

steps.title: Aggregate with sum
steps.explanation: For dot product, chain map with sum(). sum() consumes the iterator and adds all elements. Requires the item type to implement Sum.
steps.code: .map(|(x, y)| x * y).sum()

challenges.title: Index Track
challenges.description:
Use `enumerate` for index tracking. Write a function that finds all indices where a value appears in a slice. Also write a function that returns the index of the maximum value.

Expected output:
[1, 3, 5]
Some(4)
challenges.hint: enumerate yields (index, value) pairs. Filter by value match for indices, or use max_by_key to find the maximum.

steps.title: Add indices with enumerate
steps.explanation: enumerate() wraps each item with its index, yielding (usize, Item) tuples. This is essential for tracking positions.
steps.code: slice.iter().enumerate()

steps.title: Filter and map for indices
steps.explanation: Filter keeps only matching elements. map extracts just the index. The combination finds all indices where the condition holds.
steps.code:
.filter(|(_, &val)| val == target)
.map(|(idx, _)| idx)

steps.title: Use max_by_key for maximum
steps.explanation: max_by_key finds the element with maximum key value. Here the key is the value itself. map extracts the index from the (index, value) tuple.
steps.code:
.max_by_key(|(_, &val)| val)
.map(|(idx, _)| idx)

challenges.title: Flat Map
challenges.description:
Use `flat_map` to flatten and transform in one step. Write a function that takes a slice of strings and returns all words (split by whitespace) as a Vec<String>. Use flat_map to split each string and flatten results.

Expected output:
["hello", "world", "foo", "bar", "baz"]
challenges.hint: flat_map applies a function that returns an iterator, then flattens all iterators into one. Use str::split_whitespace().

steps.title: Understand flat_map
steps.explanation: flat_map is map followed by flatten. It applies a function returning an iterator, then concatenates all resulting iterators into one stream.
steps.code: sentences.iter().flat_map(|s| s.split_whitespace())

steps.title: Split each string
steps.explanation: split_whitespace() returns an iterator over &str slices. flat_map combines all these word iterators into a single stream of words.
steps.code: |s| s.split_whitespace()

steps.title: Convert to owned Strings
steps.explanation: The words are &str references. map with to_string() converts each to an owned String for the output Vec.
steps.code: .map(|w| w.to_string()).collect()

challenges.title: Take Skip While
challenges.description:
Use `take_while` and `skip_while` for conditional slicing. Write a function that extracts the "middle" of a sorted array: skip while values are negative, take while values are less than 10.

Expected output:
[0, 1, 2, 3, 4, 5]
challenges.hint: Chain skip_while(|&&x| x < 0) to skip negatives, then take_while(|&&x| x < 10) to stop at 10. Note the double reference from iter().

steps.title: Skip leading elements
steps.explanation: skip_while skips elements while the predicate is true. Once it returns false, all remaining elements are yielded.
steps.code: .skip_while(|&&x| x < 0)

steps.title: Take until condition fails
steps.explanation: take_while yields elements while the predicate is true. When it returns false, iteration stops. Combined with skip_while, this extracts a range.
steps.code: .take_while(|&&x| x < 10)

steps.title: Handle the double reference
steps.explanation: iter() yields &i32. The closure receives &&i32. Use |&&x| to pattern-match through both references to get the value.

challenges.title: Window Slide
challenges.description:
Use `windows` for sliding window iteration and `chunks` for fixed-size grouping. Write a function that computes moving averages with window size 3. Write another that sums chunks of size 2.

Expected output:
[2.0, 3.0, 4.0, 5.0]
[3, 7, 11]
challenges.hint: windows(n) yields overlapping slices of n elements. chunks(n) yields non-overlapping slices. Map each to compute average or sum.

steps.title: Sliding windows
steps.explanation: windows(n) yields overlapping slices: [1,2,3], [2,3,4], [3,4,5], etc. Each window is a &[T] of exactly n elements (last partial windows are skipped).
steps.code: nums.windows(window)

steps.title: Compute average per window
steps.explanation: Sum the window, cast to f64, divide by window size. This computes the moving average for each position.
steps.code: .map(|w| w.iter().sum::<i32>() as f64 / window as f64)

steps.title: Non-overlapping chunks
steps.explanation: chunks(n) splits into non-overlapping groups. The last chunk may be smaller. Use chunks_exact if you want to skip the last partial chunk.
steps.code: nums.chunks(chunk_size).map(|c| c.iter().sum())

challenges.title: Entry API
challenges.hint: entry(key) returns an Entry enum. or_insert(default) inserts default if missing and returns &mut to the value. Then increment it.

steps.title: Understand the entry API
steps.explanation: entry() does a single lookup that can both check and insert. It's more efficient than checking contains_key then inserting separately.
steps.code: counts.entry(key)

steps.title: Insert default if missing
steps.explanation: or_insert(0) inserts 0 if the key is missing, then returns &mut i32 to the value (new or existing). This single call handles both cases.
steps.code: .or_insert(0)

steps.title: Modify the value
steps.explanation: The returned &mut i32 can be dereferenced and incremented. The entire pattern *counts.entry(k).or_insert(0) += 1 is idiomatic for counting.
steps.code: *counts.entry(word.to_string()).or_insert(0) += 1;

challenges.title: BTree Order
challenges.description:
Use BTreeMap for sorted key iteration. Insert several key-value pairs and demonstrate that iteration is in sorted key order. Also use range() to get a subset of entries.

Expected output:
All: [(1, "one"), (2, "two"), (3, "three"), (5, "five"), (8, "eight")]
Range 2..5: [(2, "two"), (3, "three")]
challenges.hint: BTreeMap keeps keys sorted. Iterate normally for sorted order. Use map.range(2..5) to get an iterator over keys in that range.

steps.title: BTreeMap vs HashMap
steps.explanation: BTreeMap is a balanced tree that keeps keys sorted. Iteration is always in key order. HashMap is faster for random access but unordered.
steps.code: let mut map = BTreeMap::new();

steps.title: Sorted iteration
steps.explanation: Regardless of insertion order, iter() yields entries sorted by key. This is guaranteed by the B-tree structure.
steps.code: map.iter().map(|(&k, &v)| (k, v)).collect()

steps.title: Range queries
steps.explanation: range() efficiently iterates over a subset of keys. It accepts any range type: 2..5 (exclusive end), 2..=5 (inclusive), etc.
steps.code: map.range(2..5)

challenges.title: Set Ops
challenges.description:
Use HashSet for set operations. Implement functions for union, intersection, and difference of two sets. Test with sets {1,2,3,4} and {3,4,5,6}.

Expected output:
Union: [1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6]
Intersection: [3, 4]
Difference: [1, 2]
challenges.hint: HashSet has union(), intersection(), difference() methods returning iterators. Clone and collect the results.

steps.title: HashSet methods
steps.explanation: HashSet provides union, intersection, difference, and symmetric_difference. Each returns an iterator over references.
steps.code: a.union(b)

steps.title: Clone and collect
steps.explanation: These methods return iterators over &T. Use cloned() to get owned values, then collect() into a Vec.
steps.code: .cloned().collect()

steps.title: Set semantics
steps.explanation: union: all elements from both. intersection: elements in both. difference(a, b): elements in a but not b. symmetric_difference: elements in either but not both.

challenges.title: Deque Dance
challenges.description:
Use VecDeque as a double-ended queue. Implement a sliding window maximum algorithm: for each window of size k, find the maximum. Use VecDeque to track potential maximums.

Expected output:
[3, 3, 5, 5, 6, 7]
challenges.hint: Maintain a deque of indices. Remove from back while current > nums[back]. Remove from front if outside window. Front is always the max index.

steps.title: Monotonic deque approach
steps.explanation: Keep a deque of indices with decreasing values. The front always holds the index of the maximum in the current window.
steps.code: let mut deque: VecDeque<usize> = VecDeque::new();

steps.title: Maintain decreasing order
steps.explanation: Before adding a new index, remove all indices from the back whose values are smaller. This keeps the deque in decreasing order by value.

steps.title: Remove expired indices
steps.explanation: If the front index is outside the current window (more than k positions ago), remove it. The next index becomes the new maximum.

challenges.title: Heap Sort
challenges.description:
Use BinaryHeap for priority queue operations. Implement a function that returns the k largest elements from a slice using a min-heap (by negating values or using Reverse).

Expected output:
[9, 6, 5]
challenges.hint: Use a min-heap of size k. Wrap values in Reverse for min-heap behavior. Push all elements, pop excess. Convert back to sorted desc.

steps.title: BinaryHeap is a max-heap
steps.explanation: Rust's BinaryHeap is a max-heap by default. To get min-heap behavior, wrap values in Reverse from std::cmp.
steps.code: let mut heap: BinaryHeap<Reverse<i32>> = BinaryHeap::new();

steps.title: Maintain k elements
steps.explanation: Push all values. When size exceeds k, pop (removes minimum because of Reverse). After processing all values, heap contains k largest.

steps.title: Extract and sort results
steps.explanation: Convert heap to iterator, unwrap Reverse to get original values, collect and sort descending for ordered output.
steps.code: heap.into_iter().map(|Reverse(x)| x).collect()

challenges.title: String Craft
challenges.description:
Practice advanced string manipulation. Write `to_title_case(s: &str) -> String` that capitalizes the first letter of each word. Handle multiple spaces and edge cases.

Expected output:
Hello World
The Quick Brown Fox
challenges.hint: Split by whitespace, capitalize each word (first char uppercase, rest lowercase), join with space. Use chars() and iterators.

steps.title: Split into words
steps.explanation: split_whitespace() handles multiple spaces and trims. It yields word slices without empty strings.
steps.code: s.split_whitespace()

steps.title: Capitalize first char
steps.explanation: Get the first char with chars().next(). Use to_uppercase() which returns an iterator (some chars map to multiple uppercase chars). Chain with remaining chars lowercased.
steps.code: first.to_uppercase().chain(chars.flat_map(|c| c.to_lowercase()))

steps.title: Rejoin words
steps.explanation: Collect transformed words into a Vec, then join with single space. This normalizes whitespace in the output.
steps.code: .collect::<Vec<_>>().join(" ")

challenges.title: From Parse
challenges.description:
Implement `FromStr` for a custom type. Create `Point { x: i32, y: i32 }` and implement FromStr to parse "x,y" format. Return a custom error for invalid input.

Expected output:
Ok(Point { x: 10, y: 20 })
Err("Invalid format")
challenges.hint: Split by comma, parse both parts as i32. Use ? or map_err to convert ParseIntError to String. Return descriptive errors.

steps.title: Define FromStr trait
steps.explanation: FromStr requires implementing from_str and defining an Err type. Once implemented, you can use .parse::<Point>() on strings.

steps.title: Split and validate
steps.explanation: Split by comma, check we have exactly 2 parts. Return error early if format is wrong.

steps.title: Parse with error conversion
steps.explanation: Parse each part as i32. Use map_err to convert ParseIntError to our String error type. The ? operator propagates errors.
steps.code: let x = parts[0].trim().parse::<i32>().map_err(|_| "Invalid format".to_string())?;

challenges.title: Derive Power
challenges.description:
Use derive macros effectively. Create a `Person` struct with name and age. Derive Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Hash. Create two identical persons, compare them, clone one, and use one as a HashMap key.

Expected output:
Equal: true
Clone: Person { name: "Alice", age: 30 }
Found in map: true
challenges.hint: Add #[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Hash)] before struct. This auto-generates implementations based on fields.

steps.title: Derive common traits
steps.explanation: Debug enables {:?} formatting. Clone enables .clone(). PartialEq/Eq enable == comparison. Hash enables use as HashMap key.
steps.code: #[derive(Debug, Clone, PartialEq, Eq, Hash)]

steps.title: Trait requirements
steps.explanation: HashMap keys require Hash + Eq. BTreeMap keys require Ord. The derive macro generates implementations that work field-by-field.
steps.code: map.insert(p1, "person1");

steps.title: Structural equality
steps.explanation: Derived PartialEq compares all fields. Two Person values are equal if name and age both match. This is used for both == and HashMap lookup.

challenges.title: Error Custom
challenges.description:
Create a custom error enum implementing std::error::Error. Define `ParseError` with variants `Empty`, `InvalidNumber(String)`, `TooManyParts`. Implement Display and Error traits. Use it in a parsing function.

Expected output:
Ok(42)
Err(Empty)
Err(InvalidNumber("abc"))
challenges.hint: Implement Display with match on variants. parse_single_number checks for empty, then parses, returning appropriate errors.

steps.title: Define error variants
steps.explanation: Use an enum to represent different error cases. Include data in variants that need context (like the invalid input string).

steps.title: Implement Display
steps.explanation: Display provides human-readable error messages. Match on variants and format appropriately. This is used by {} and error messages.

steps.title: Implement Error trait
steps.explanation: std::error::Error requires Debug + Display. The default implementations suffice for simple errors. Override source() for error chaining.
steps.code: impl Error for ParseError {}

challenges.title: Const Generic
challenges.description:
Use const generics for compile-time array sizes. Create a `Matrix<T, const ROWS: usize, const COLS: usize>` that wraps a 2D array. Implement new() and get(row, col). Print elements from a 2x3 matrix.

Expected output:
1 2 3
4 5 6
challenges.hint: const generics let you parameterize by values, not just types. Use [[T; COLS]; ROWS] for fixed-size 2D array. T: Default lets you initialize.

steps.title: Const generics syntax
steps.explanation: const ROWS: usize is a const generic parameter. The type includes the size: Matrix<i32, 2, 3> is different from Matrix<i32, 3, 2>.
steps.code: struct Matrix<T, const ROWS: usize, const COLS: usize>

steps.title: Fixed-size array initialization
steps.explanation: With T: Copy + Default, we can initialize arrays using [T::default(); N]. This creates a ROWS x COLS array filled with default values.
steps.code: data: [[T::default(); COLS]; ROWS]

steps.title: Compile-time size checking
steps.explanation: The dimensions are part of the type. Matrix<i32, 2, 3> and Matrix<i32, 3, 2> are incompatible types. Size errors are caught at compile time.

challenges.title: Assoc Type
challenges.description:
Use associated types in traits. Define trait `Container` with associated type `Item` and methods `add(&mut self, item: Self::Item)` and `get(&self) -> Option<&Self::Item>`. Implement for a wrapper around Vec that only stores the last added item.

Expected output:
None
Some(3)
challenges.hint: Associated types specify types that are determined by the implementor. type Item = T; in the impl block binds the associated type to T.

steps.title: Define associated type
steps.explanation: type Item; in the trait declares an associated type. Unlike generic parameters, each type can only implement the trait once (with one Item type).

steps.title: Bind in implementation
steps.explanation: The impl specifies what Item is for this type. For LastOnly<T>, Item is T. Self::Item refers to this bound type throughout the impl.

steps.title: Use in method signatures
steps.explanation: Methods use Self::Item to refer to the associated type. This provides type safety - add() accepts the right type, get() returns the right type.
steps.code:
fn add(&mut self, item: Self::Item)
fn get(&self) -> Option<&Self::Item>

challenges.title: Where Bound
challenges.description:
Use `where` clauses for complex trait bounds. Write a function `print_if_debug<T>(item: T)` that prints the item only if T implements Debug. Use a separate function `compare_and_print<T>(a: T, b: T)` where T: Debug + PartialOrd that compares and prints the larger one.

Expected output:
Item: 42
Larger: world
challenges.hint: where clauses make complex bounds readable. Use {:?} for Debug printing. Use > or < for PartialOrd comparison.

steps.title: where clause syntax
steps.explanation: where clauses separate bounds from the function signature, improving readability for complex bounds. They're equivalent to inline bounds but cleaner.

steps.title: Multiple trait bounds
steps.explanation: Use + to require multiple traits. T: Debug + PartialOrd means T must implement both. The function can use methods from both traits.
steps.code:
where
    T: Debug + PartialOrd,

steps.title: Use the bounded traits
steps.explanation: With Debug, use {:?} formatting. With PartialOrd, use comparison operators. The compiler ensures these are available.
steps.code:
if a > b { ... }
println!("Larger: {:?}", a);

challenges.title: Supertrait
challenges.description:
Use supertraits to require other trait implementations. Define trait `Printable: Display + Debug` that has a method `print_both(&self)`. Types implementing Printable must also implement Display and Debug. Implement for a simple struct.

Expected output:
Display: Value(42)
Debug: Value(42)
challenges.hint: Supertraits are specified after the colon. Methods in the trait can use methods from supertraits. Default implementations in traits are inherited.

steps.title: Define supertrait bounds
steps.explanation: trait Printable: Display + Debug means Printable requires Display and Debug. You can't implement Printable without implementing both.
steps.code: trait Printable: Display + Debug

steps.title: Use supertraits in default methods
steps.explanation: The print_both method can use {} (Display) and {:?} (Debug) because the supertraits guarantee these are implemented.

steps.title: Empty impl uses defaults
steps.explanation: impl Printable for Value {} is valid because we've implemented both supertraits. The default print_both is automatically available.
steps.code: impl Printable for Value {}

challenges.title: Blanket Impl
challenges.description:
Create a blanket implementation. Define trait `Stringify` with method `stringify(&self) -> String`. Implement it for all types that implement Display. Test with i32, String, and a custom type.

Expected output:
42
hello
Point(1, 2)
challenges.hint: Blanket implementations use impl<T: Trait> MyTrait for T. This implements MyTrait for all types satisfying the bound.

steps.title: Blanket implementation syntax
steps.explanation: impl<T: Display> Stringify for T implements Stringify for every type T that implements Display. This is a blanket implementation.
steps.code: impl<T: Display> Stringify for T

steps.title: Use the bound trait
steps.explanation: Inside the implementation, we can use Display's functionality. format!("{}", self) works because T: Display.

steps.title: Automatic coverage
steps.explanation: Any type implementing Display automatically gets Stringify. This includes built-in types (i32, String) and custom types with Display impl.

challenges.title: Trait Extend
challenges.description:
Use extension traits to add methods to foreign types. Create trait `VecExt<T>` with method `second(&self) -> Option<&T>`. Implement it for Vec<T> to get the second element. Test with vectors of different lengths.

Expected output:
Some(2)
None
challenges.hint: This works because our trait is local, even though Vec is foreign. Use self.get(1) to safely access the second element.

steps.title: Extension trait pattern
steps.explanation: The orphan rule prevents implementing foreign traits on foreign types. But we can define a new local trait and implement it for foreign types.

steps.title: Implement for foreign type
steps.explanation: We can implement VecExt (local) for Vec<T> (foreign). This adds methods to Vec that feel like native methods.
steps.code: impl<T> VecExt<T> for Vec<T>

steps.title: Use safe accessors
steps.explanation: self.get(1) returns Option<&T>, handling out-of-bounds safely. This is better than self[1] which would panic.

challenges.title: Dispatch Demo
challenges.description:
Compare static dispatch (generics) vs dynamic dispatch (trait objects). Create trait `Speak` with `speak(&self) -> String`. Implement for Dog and Cat. Write both static `speak_static<T: Speak>(s: &T)` and dynamic `speak_dynamic(s: &dyn Speak)` functions.

Expected output:
Static: Woof!
Static: Meow!
Dynamic: Woof!
Dynamic: Meow!
challenges.hint: Static dispatch uses generics (monomorphization at compile time). Dynamic dispatch uses trait objects (vtable lookup at runtime). Both call the same methods.

steps.title: Static dispatch with generics
steps.explanation: fn speak_static<T: Speak> generates a separate function for each concrete type at compile time. No runtime overhead, but more code size.
steps.code: fn speak_static<T: Speak>(s: &T)

steps.title: Dynamic dispatch with dyn
steps.explanation: fn speak_dynamic(s: &dyn Speak) uses a trait object. Method calls go through a vtable at runtime. One function handles all types.
steps.code: fn speak_dynamic(s: &dyn Speak)

steps.title: Trade-offs
steps.explanation: Static: faster (no vtable lookup), enables inlining, but code bloat. Dynamic: smaller code, works with heterogeneous collections, but slight runtime cost.

challenges.title: Marker Trait
challenges.description:
Use marker traits to categorize types. Create empty marker traits `Safe` and `Validated`. Create structs `UserInput` and `SanitizedInput` where only SanitizedInput implements Safe. Write a function that only accepts Safe types.

Expected output:
Processing safe data: clean_data
challenges.hint: Marker traits have no methods but constrain which types can be used. They encode type-level properties that the compiler can check.

steps.title: Define empty marker trait
steps.explanation: trait Safe {} has no methods. Its only purpose is to mark types. This is a type-level tag that the compiler enforces.
steps.code: trait Safe {}

steps.title: Implement selectively
steps.explanation: Only SanitizedInput implements Safe. UserInput doesn't. This creates a compile-time distinction between sanitized and unsanitized data.
steps.code: impl Safe for SanitizedInput {}

steps.title: Enforce at compile time
steps.explanation: process_safe requires T: Safe. Passing UserInput won't compile. This prevents bugs from using unvalidated data in sensitive operations.
steps.code: fn process_safe<T: Safe>(input: &T, data: &str)

challenges.title: Send Sync
challenges.description:
Demonstrate Send and Sync traits for thread safety. Create a thread-safe counter using Arc<Mutex<i32>>. Show that Rc<RefCell<i32>> cannot be sent between threads by commenting out the failing code.

Expected output:
Arc<Mutex> count: 10
challenges.hint: Send: safe to transfer ownership between threads. Sync: safe to share references between threads. Arc is Send+Sync, Rc is neither.

steps.title: Send trait
steps.explanation: Send means ownership can be transferred between threads. Most types are Send. Rc is not Send because reference count updates aren't atomic.
steps.code: // Arc<T> is Send if T: Send + Sync

steps.title: Sync trait
steps.explanation: Sync means &T can be shared between threads. T: Sync implies &T: Send. Mutex makes inner types Sync-safe by providing synchronized access.
steps.code: // Mutex<T> is Sync if T: Send

steps.title: Arc<Mutex<T>> pattern
steps.explanation: Arc provides thread-safe reference counting (Send+Sync). Mutex provides thread-safe interior mutability. Combined, they enable shared mutable state across threads.
steps.code: let counter = Arc::new(Mutex::new(0));

challenges.title: Unsafe Peek
challenges.description:
Understand basic unsafe operations. Write a function that uses unsafe to read from a raw pointer. Create a safe wrapper that converts a reference to a pointer and back. Demonstrate with a simple example.

Expected output:
Value via safe ref: 42
Value via unsafe ptr: 42
challenges.hint: Raw pointers (*const T, *mut T) can be created safely, but dereferencing requires unsafe. The unsafe block tells the compiler you're taking responsibility for safety.

steps.title: Create raw pointer safely
steps.explanation: Converting a reference to a raw pointer is safe. No unsafe block needed. The pointer is just an address.
steps.code: let ptr: *const i32 = value;

steps.title: Dereference in unsafe block
steps.explanation: Dereferencing a raw pointer requires unsafe because the compiler can't verify the pointer is valid. You're asserting it's safe.
steps.code: unsafe { *ptr }

steps.title: Safe wrapper pattern
steps.explanation: safe_read takes a reference (guaranteed valid) and creates a pointer from it. Since we control the pointer's origin, the unsafe deref is actually safe. This is how safe abstractions wrap unsafe operations.

challenges.title: Memoize Cache
challenges.description:
Implement memoization using a closure with captured state. Create a memoized Fibonacci function using a HashMap to cache results. Count and display cache hits.

Expected output:
fib(10) = 55
fib(10) = 55 (cached)
Cache size: 11
challenges.hint: The closure captures cache by move. Check if n is in cache; if so, return cached value. Otherwise compute (recursively or iteratively), store, and return.

steps.title: Capture mutable state
steps.explanation: The closure captures cache by moving it into the closure. FnMut allows mutation of captured variables.
steps.code:
let mut cache: HashMap<u64, u64> = HashMap::new();
move |n: u64| -> u64

steps.title: Check cache first
steps.explanation: Before computing, check if the result is already cached. If so, return it immediately without recomputation.

steps.title: Compute and cache
steps.explanation: If not cached, compute the result (iteratively to avoid stack overflow), store it in the cache, then return it.
steps.code:
cache.insert(n, result);
(result, cache.len())

challenges.title: Lazy Eval
challenges.description:
Create a lazy evaluation wrapper. Implement `Lazy<T>` that takes a closure and only evaluates it on first access, caching the result. Use Cell/RefCell for interior mutability.

Expected output:
Computing expensive value...
First access: 42
Second access: 42
challenges.hint: Store the closure in Option to allow taking it out (FnOnce). On first get, take the closure, call it, store the result. Return a Ref to the cached value.

steps.title: Store closure and result
steps.explanation: Use RefCell<Option<T>> for the value and RefCell<Option<F>> for the closure. Option allows us to take() the FnOnce closure.

steps.title: Evaluate on first access
steps.explanation: Check if value is None. If so, take() the closure from init (consuming it), call it, store the result. This happens only once.

steps.title: Return reference to cached
steps.explanation: Use Ref::map to transform Ref<Option<T>> into Ref<T>. This maintains the borrow through the return.
steps.code: Ref::map(self.value.borrow(), |opt| opt.as_ref().unwrap())

challenges.title: Type State
challenges.description:
Use the typestate pattern for compile-time state machine validation. Create a `Connection` type that can only call `connect()` when Disconnected and `send()` when Connected. Use PhantomData and zero-sized types.

Expected output:
Connecting to server...
Sending: Hello!
Disconnecting...
challenges.hint: Different impl blocks for different states. Methods consume self and return new state. PhantomData<State> makes Connection<Disconnected> different from Connection<Connected>.

steps.title: Define state marker types
steps.explanation: Disconnected and Connected are zero-sized types (ZSTs). They exist only at compile time to distinguish states.
steps.code:
struct Disconnected;
struct Connected;

steps.title: Parameterize by state
steps.explanation: Connection<State> uses PhantomData<State> to "use" the type parameter. Connection<Disconnected> and Connection<Connected> are different types.

steps.title: State-specific implementations
steps.explanation: impl Connection<Disconnected> has connect(). impl Connection<Connected> has send() and disconnect(). The compiler prevents calling methods in wrong states.

challenges.title: Recursive List
challenges.description:
Create a recursive linked list type using Box. Define `enum List { Nil, Cons(i32, Box<List>) }`. Implement methods to create, append, and iterate. Build a list [1, 2, 3] and sum its elements.

Expected output:
Sum: 6
challenges.hint: Box provides indirection, giving List a known size. prepend creates Cons with current list boxed. sum uses recursion or a loop.

steps.title: Box for recursive types
steps.explanation: Without Box, List would have infinite size. Box<List> is a pointer with known size, allowing the recursion.

steps.title: Prepend by boxing current
steps.explanation: prepend takes ownership of the current list, boxes it, and creates a new Cons node pointing to it.

steps.title: Recursive sum
steps.explanation: Pattern match on Nil (base case: 0) and Cons (add value to recursive sum of rest). Box auto-derefs, so rest.sum() works.

challenges.title: Range Iter
challenges.description:
Implement a custom range iterator from scratch. Create `MyRange { current: i32, end: i32 }` that yields numbers from current to end (exclusive). Implement Iterator trait. Use it in a for loop.

Expected output:
1
2
3
4
challenges.hint: In next(), check if current < end. If so, save current, increment it, return Some(saved). Otherwise return None.

steps.title: Track iteration state
steps.explanation: MyRange tracks current position and end. Each call to next() advances current until it reaches end.

steps.title: Implement Iterator trait
steps.explanation: Define Item type as i32. next() returns Option<i32> - Some when values remain, None when exhausted.

steps.title: Advance and return
steps.explanation: Check bounds, save current value, increment, return Some. Returning None signals iteration end.

challenges.title: Double End
challenges.description:
Implement `DoubleEndedIterator` for a custom type. Create a wrapper around a Vec that can iterate from both ends. Implement both `next()` and `next_back()`. Demonstrate with a sequence.

Expected output:
Front: 1, Back: 4
Front: 2, Back: 3
challenges.hint: Track front and back indices. next() advances front, next_back() decrements back. Stop when front >= back.

steps.title: Track both ends
steps.explanation: front starts at 0, back starts at len. They move toward each other. When front >= back, iteration is complete.
steps.code: BothEnds { data, front: 0, back: len }

steps.title: Implement next()
steps.explanation: Return element at front index, then increment front. Check front < back to ensure we haven't crossed.

steps.title: Implement next_back()
steps.explanation: Decrement back first (since it starts past the last element), then return element at back. Same bounds check.

challenges.title: Into Iter
challenges.description:
Implement `IntoIterator` for a custom collection. Create `Stack<T>` with push/pop. Implement IntoIterator to consume the stack and yield elements. Demonstrate in a for loop.

Expected output:
3
2
1
challenges.hint: IntoIterator allows for loops on custom types. Reverse the Vec to get LIFO order, then call into_iter() on it.

steps.title: Define IntoIterator types
steps.explanation: Item is what we yield. IntoIter is the iterator type returned. We reuse Vec's IntoIter for simplicity.
steps.code:
type Item = T;
type IntoIter = std::vec::IntoIter<T>;

steps.title: Consume and transform
steps.explanation: into_iter takes self by value (consuming it). Reverse for LIFO order, then delegate to Vec's into_iter.

steps.title: Enable for loops
steps.explanation: With IntoIterator implemented, for item in stack works. The stack is consumed, and items are yielded in LIFO order.

challenges.title: Index Op
challenges.description:
Implement `Index` and `IndexMut` traits for custom indexing. Create `Grid<T>` that stores a 2D array and allows indexing with (row, col) tuples. Implement both read and write access.

Expected output:
0
42
challenges.hint: Convert (row, col) to linear index: row * cols + col. Return reference to that element in the data Vec.

steps.title: Store 2D as 1D
steps.explanation: Store data in a flat Vec. Calculate linear index from (row, col) using row * cols + col. This is cache-friendly.
steps.code: data: vec![T::default(); rows * cols]

steps.title: Implement Index
steps.explanation: Index trait enables grid[(r, c)] syntax for reading. Output is T, and we return a reference to the element.

steps.title: Implement IndexMut
steps.explanation: IndexMut enables grid[(r, c)] = value for writing. Returns &mut, allowing assignment to the indexed location.

challenges.title: Add Overload
challenges.hint: Add::add takes self and other, returns Output. AddAssign::add_assign takes &mut self and modifies in place.

steps.title: Implement Add
steps.explanation: Add trait enables + operator. Output is the result type. add() consumes both operands and returns a new Vector2D.

steps.title: Implement AddAssign
steps.explanation: AddAssign enables += operator. add_assign takes &mut self and modifies in place. No return value needed.

steps.title: Copy for operator ergonomics
steps.explanation: Deriving Copy allows operators to work without consuming values unexpectedly. Small types like Vector2D are good Copy candidates.

challenges.title: Deref Coerce
challenges.description:
Implement `Deref` for smart pointer behavior. Create `MyBox<T>` that wraps a value. Implement Deref so it auto-dereferences to &T. Demonstrate deref coercion with a function taking &str.

Expected output:
Hello, Rust!
challenges.hint: Deref::deref returns a reference to the inner value. Deref coercion chains automatically: &MyBox<T> -> &T.

steps.title: Implement Deref
steps.explanation: Deref trait enables * operator and auto-deref. Target is what we dereference to. deref() returns &Target.

steps.title: Deref coercion chain
steps.explanation: When &MyBox<String> is passed where &str is expected, Rust coerces: &MyBox<String> -> &String -> &str. This happens automatically.
steps.code: greet(&boxed);  // &MyBox<String> coerces to &str

steps.title: Ergonomic smart pointers
steps.explanation: Deref makes smart pointers transparent. Methods on T can be called directly on MyBox<T> due to auto-deref. This is how Box, Rc, Arc work.

challenges.title: Ord Compare
challenges.description:
Implement `Ord` and `PartialOrd` for custom sorting. Create `Student { name: String, grade: u32 }`. Sort by grade descending, then by name ascending for ties. Demonstrate with a vector of students.

Expected output:
Alice: 95
Charlie: 95
Bob: 85
challenges.hint: Compare grades with reverse() for descending. Use then_with() to compare names only when grades are equal.

steps.title: Ord for total ordering
steps.explanation: Ord::cmp returns Ordering (Less, Equal, Greater). For descending grade, compare other to self instead of self to other.
steps.code: other.grade.cmp(&self.grade)

steps.title: Secondary sort with then_with
steps.explanation: then_with() only evaluates if the first comparison is Equal. This adds name as a tiebreaker, ascending (self.name vs other.name).
steps.code: .then_with(|| self.name.cmp(&other.name))

steps.title: PartialOrd delegation
steps.explanation: For types with total ordering, PartialOrd::partial_cmp just wraps Ord::cmp in Some. This is the standard pattern.

challenges.title: Try From
challenges.description:
Implement `TryFrom` for fallible conversions. Create `Email` struct. Implement TryFrom<&str> that validates the email contains @ and returns Error if invalid.

Expected output:
Ok(Email { address: "user@example.com" })
Err("Invalid email: missing @")
challenges.hint: Check if the string contains '@'. If yes, create Email. If no, return Err with a descriptive message.

steps.title: TryFrom for fallible conversion
steps.explanation: TryFrom is like From but can fail. It returns Result<Self, Self::Error>. Use for conversions that might not succeed.

steps.title: Validate and construct
steps.explanation: Check the validation condition. Return Ok with the constructed value on success, Err with an error message on failure.

steps.title: TryInto is automatic
steps.explanation: Implementing TryFrom<T> for U automatically provides TryInto<U> for T. So "str".try_into() works to get Email.
steps.code: let valid: Result<Email, _> = "user@example.com".try_into();

challenges.title: Bitwise Ops
challenges.description:
Implement bitwise operations for a flags struct. Create `Permissions` with Read, Write, Execute flags. Use BitOr, BitAnd, and Not traits to combine and check permissions.

Expected output:
Read | Write: Permissions(3)
Has Read: true
Complement: Permissions(252)
challenges.hint: Use the corresponding bitwise operators on the inner u8. For contains, use & and check if result equals the flag.

steps.title: Define flag constants
steps.explanation: Use powers of 2 for flags so they can be combined with bitwise OR. 1=Read, 2=Write, 4=Execute allows any combination.

steps.title: Implement BitOr
steps.explanation: | combines flags. Apply | to the inner u8 values to set bits from both operands.

steps.title: Check containment
steps.explanation: contains checks if all bits in other are set in self. Use & and compare to other (not to non-zero).

challenges.title: Async Basic
challenges.description:
Write a simple async function that simulates work. Create `async fn compute(x: i32) -> i32` that returns x * 2. Use block_on from a simple executor to run it.

Expected output:
Result: 84
challenges.hint: async fn returns impl Future. The computation is trivial here - just return x * 2. block_on polls the future until Ready.

steps.title: async fn basics
steps.explanation: async fn automatically returns impl Future. The body is transformed into a state machine. For simple computations, it's immediately ready.

steps.title: Poll mechanism
steps.explanation: Futures are polled by an executor. Poll::Ready(value) means done. Poll::Pending means not ready yet - the executor should wait and poll again later.

steps.title: Simple executor
steps.explanation: block_on is a minimal executor that busy-loops polling. Real executors are smarter, using Waker to sleep until progress is possible.

challenges.title: Pin Unpin
challenges.description:
Demonstrate Pin for self-referential data. Create a struct that prints whether it's Unpin. Show that most types are Unpin by default.

Expected output:
i32 is Unpin: true
String is Unpin: true
Vec<i32> is Unpin: true
challenges.hint: Most types implement Unpin automatically. Only self-referential types (like some async state machines) are !Unpin. is_unpin works because of the bound.

steps.title: Unpin marker trait
steps.explanation: Unpin indicates a type can be safely moved after being pinned. Most types auto-implement Unpin. Only types with internal pointers to themselves need !Unpin.

steps.title: Compile-time bounds
steps.explanation: is_unpin<T: Unpin>() only compiles when called with types implementing Unpin. The function body doesn't matter - the bound is the check.

steps.title: Pin's purpose
steps.explanation: Pin prevents moving data that has self-references. For Unpin types, Pin<&mut T> is equivalent to &mut T. For !Unpin types, Pin guarantees the data won't move.

challenges.title: Static Life
challenges.description:
Work with 'static lifetime. Create a function that returns &'static str. Demonstrate string literals are 'static. Show how to create 'static data with Box::leak.

Expected output:
Literal: hello
Leaked: dynamic static
challenges.hint: String literals have 'static lifetime. Box::leak converts Box<T> to &'static mut T by intentionally leaking the memory.

steps.title: String literals are 'static
steps.explanation: String literals are stored in the binary's read-only memory and live for the entire program. They automatically have 'static lifetime.

steps.title: Box::leak for dynamic 'static
steps.explanation: Box::leak intentionally leaks memory, converting owned data to a 'static reference. Use sparingly - the memory is never freed.
steps.code: Box::leak(s.into_boxed_str())

steps.title: 'static meaning
steps.explanation: 'static means the reference is valid for the entire program duration. It doesn't mean the data is immutable or stored statically - just that it lives long enough.

challenges.title: HRTB
challenges.description:
Use Higher-Ranked Trait Bounds (for<'a>). Write a function that accepts any closure that can work with any lifetime. Demonstrate with a closure that takes a reference.

Expected output:
Result: 6
challenges.hint: for<'a> means 'for any lifetime a'. The closure must work with references of any lifetime, not just a specific one.

steps.title: HRTB syntax
steps.explanation: for<'a> Fn(&'a i32) -> i32 means F must implement Fn for ALL possible lifetimes 'a, not just one specific lifetime.
steps.code: F: for<'a> Fn(&'a i32) -> i32

steps.title: Why HRTB needed
steps.explanation: Without HRTB, the lifetime would be tied to the function's signature. HRTB allows the closure to work with references created inside the function.

steps.title: Common use
steps.explanation: HRTB is often used with closures taking references, Iterator methods, and other generic code where the exact lifetime isn't known until call time.

challenges.title: Variance
challenges.description:
Demonstrate type variance. Show that &'a T is covariant in 'a - a longer lifetime can be used where a shorter one is expected.

Expected output:
Works: hello
challenges.hint: Covariance means subtypes can replace supertypes. 'static is a subtype of all lifetimes, so &'static str works where &'short str is needed.

steps.title: Lifetime subtyping
steps.explanation: 'static is a subtype of all other lifetimes. If something lives 'static, it certainly lives for any shorter duration too.
steps.code: let long_lived: &'static str = "hello";

steps.title: Covariance of &'a T
steps.explanation: References are covariant in their lifetime parameter. &'static T can be used where &'short T is expected - the reference lives longer than required, which is safe.

steps.title: Variance rules
steps.explanation: &T is covariant in T and 'a. &mut T is invariant in T. fn(T) is contravariant in T. These rules ensure soundness.

challenges.title: Reborrow
challenges.description:
Demonstrate reborrowing of mutable references. Show how to pass &mut through multiple function calls without moving it.

Expected output:
First: 1
Second: 2
Final: 2
challenges.hint: When passing &mut to a function, Rust automatically reborrows - it creates a new &mut from the existing one, keeping the original valid.

steps.title: Automatic reborrowing
steps.explanation: When you pass &mut to a function, Rust doesn't move the reference. It reborrows - creating a temporary &mut that expires after the call.
steps.code:
increment(r);
// r is still valid here

steps.title: Why this works
steps.explanation: Reborrowing is like: increment(&mut *r). This creates a new &mut from r for the call duration. After the call, r becomes usable again.

steps.title: vs moving
steps.explanation: If &mut moved like other types, you couldn't use r after passing it once. Reborrowing allows sequential use of the same mutable reference.

challenges.title: Enum Size
challenges.description:
Explore enum memory layout. Create enums with different variant sizes and print their sizes. Demonstrate null pointer optimization with Option<Box<T>>.

Expected output:
Small enum: 1 byte
Mixed enum: 16 bytes
Option<Box>: 8 bytes (same as Box!)
challenges.hint: Enums are sized to fit their largest variant plus a discriminant. Option<NonNull types> uses null pointer optimization - None is represented as null.

steps.title: Discriminant
steps.explanation: Enums need to store which variant they are. Small only needs 1 byte to distinguish 2 variants.
steps.code: enum Small { A, B }  // 1 byte

steps.title: Size is largest variant + discriminant
steps.explanation: Mixed must fit String (24 bytes typically) plus alignment. The discriminant fits in padding or extra space.

steps.title: Null pointer optimization
steps.explanation: Option<Box<T>> is the same size as Box<T>! Since Box can't be null, Option uses null to represent None. This is a major optimization.
steps.code: size_of::<Option<Box<i32>>>() == size_of::<Box<i32>>()

challenges.title: Trait Alias
challenges.description:
Create a trait alias using a blanket implementation. Define trait `StringLike` that's automatically implemented for anything that implements Display + Debug + Clone.

Expected output:
Printing: hello
Debug: "hello"
challenges.hint: The supertrait declaration plus blanket impl effectively creates a trait alias. Any type with the supertraits automatically gets StringLike.

steps.title: Supertrait declaration
steps.explanation: trait StringLike: Display + Debug + Clone declares that implementors must also implement the supertraits. This is half of the 'alias'.
steps.code: trait StringLike: Display + Debug + Clone {}

steps.title: Blanket implementation
steps.explanation: impl<T: Display + Debug + Clone> StringLike for T {} automatically implements StringLike for any type with the required traits. This completes the alias.
steps.code: impl<T: Display + Debug + Clone> StringLike for T {}

steps.title: Usage simplification
steps.explanation: Now T: StringLike is shorter than T: Display + Debug + Clone. Functions can use the alias, and the supertraits are available.

challenges.title: Module Privacy
challenges.description:
Demonstrate Rust's module privacy rules. Create a module with private fields, a public constructor, and controlled access. Show the difference between pub, pub(crate), and pub(super).

Expected output:
Secret created
Public value: 42
Crate value: hidden
challenges.hint: pub: visible everywhere. pub(crate): visible in current crate. pub(super): visible in parent module. Default (no keyword): private to module.

steps.title: Visibility levels
steps.explanation: pub: everywhere. pub(crate): same crate only. pub(super): parent module. pub(in path): specific ancestor. No modifier: current module only.
steps.code:
pub public_val: i32,
pub(crate) crate_val: String,
private_val: String,

steps.title: Constructor pattern
steps.explanation: With private fields, direct struct construction is impossible outside the module. Provide a pub fn new() for controlled construction.

steps.title: Accessor methods
steps.explanation: To expose private data, provide pub methods like reveal(). This allows read access while preventing modification.

challenges.title: DST Slice
challenges.description:
Work with Dynamically Sized Types (DSTs). Demonstrate that [T] and str are DSTs that must be behind a pointer. Show how to work with slices.

Expected output:
Slice len: 5
Str len: 5
Sum: 15
challenges.hint: DSTs have unknown size at compile time. &[T] and &str are fat pointers containing data pointer + length. This enables working with variable-length data.

steps.title: DSTs explained
steps.explanation: [T] and str are unsized types - their size isn't known at compile time. You can't have them directly on the stack. They must be behind a pointer.
steps.code:
// [i32] is a DST
// &[i32] is a fat pointer to a DST

steps.title: Fat pointers
steps.explanation: &[T] is 2 words: pointer to data + length. &str is the same. This extra word is what makes DSTs usable - the length is carried at runtime.
steps.code: slice.len()  // length is stored in the fat pointer

steps.title: Coercion
steps.explanation: &[T; N] coerces to &[T] automatically. The fixed-size array reference becomes a fat pointer with the length encoded.
steps.code: slice_info(&arr)  // &[i32; 5] coerces to &[i32]
